evals.prompt: I need to design the API for a blog platform. We have users, posts, comments, and tags. Posts can have multiple tags. Comments are threaded (replies to other comments). Users can be authors or admins. The frontend is a Next.js app. Design the REST API.
evals.expected_output: Should identify all resources, design endpoints with proper RESTful naming, define request/response schemas, standardize errors, and include pagination for list endpoints.
assertions: Identifies all resources (users, posts, comments, tags)
Uses plural nouns in URLs (/posts, /comments, /tags)
Handles the M:N relationship for posts and tags appropriately
Designs threaded comments without excessive URL nesting
Defines standard error response format
Includes pagination for list endpoints
Specifies auth requirements per endpoint
Distinguishes author vs admin permissions
Produces a complete API specification document

evals.prompt: design api endpoints for user registration, login, password reset, and profile management
evals.expected_output: Should design auth-related endpoints following security best practices, not expose sensitive information in responses, and use appropriate HTTP methods.
assertions: POST for registration, login, and password reset (not GET)
Does not return password hash or sensitive data in responses
Includes email verification flow
Password reset uses a token-based flow (not sending password via email)
Login response includes token and token expiry
Profile endpoints require authentication
Standardized error responses for auth failures (401 vs 403)

evals.prompt:
We have an existing API with these endpoints:
GET /getUsers
GET /getUserById?id=123
POST /createUser
POST /updateUser
GET /deleteUser?id=123

This doesn't feel right. Can you redesign it following proper REST conventions?
evals.expected_output: Should identify specific violations (verbs in URLs, GET for delete, query params for identity) and redesign using proper REST conventions.
assertions: Identifies that URLs use verbs instead of nouns
Identifies that DELETE uses GET method
Identifies that identity is in query params instead of path
Redesigns to: GET /users, GET /users/:id, POST /users, PATCH /users/:id, DELETE /users/:id
Explains WHY each change matters (not just 'this is the convention')
Notes that this is a breaking change requiring versioning or migration plan
